prompt:
We are migrating a few hundred checked-in docs and config snippets (intranet URLs, renamed keys, that sort of thing) and I am done hand-running sed and hoping. I want a small batch editor with guard rails: `fixup.sh` in the repo root, plain bash + sed + diff, portable flags only (some of the team is on macOS).

Usage: `fixup.sh [--dry-run] <sed-expression> <glob>`. The expression is a single sed script like `s|old|new|g`, passed through to sed untouched. The glob comes in QUOTED and the tool expands it itself, relative to the current directory; matches are processed in ascending C byte order. Only regular files count, and anything ending in `.bak` is skipped no matter what the glob catches — the tool must never chew on its own backups. A file is "changed" when applying the expression would alter its bytes.

--dry-run: for each file that would change, print a unified diff of current vs would-be content — exactly `diff -u` output with the labels `<file>` and `<file> (fixed)` (so the header lines are `--- <file>` and `+++ <file> (fixed)`, no timestamps) — then one summary line `would change N of M file(s)`. It must not touch the tree at all: no edits, no backups, no scratch files left anywhere. Exit 0 when nothing would change, 1 when at least one file would (CI keys off that, git-diff style).

Real run: for each file that would change, first copy the original aside as `<file>.bak`, then rewrite the file in place, printing `fixed: <file>`. Files that would not change are left completely alone — no backup, no rewrite. Finish with `changed N of M file(s)` and exit 0. Running the same fixup twice must be safe: the second run reports `changed 0 of M file(s)` and not a byte moves, backups included.

Validate the expression BEFORE touching anything: if sed rejects it, print `fixup.sh: bad expression: <expression>` to stderr and exit 65 with the tree untouched (sed's own error text stays off the terminal). A glob that matches no usable file: `fixup.sh: no files match: <glob>` to stderr, exit 66. Anything wrong with the invocation itself (missing args, unknown flag): `usage: fixup.sh [--dry-run] <sed-expression> <glob>` to stderr, exit 64. Diffs and per-file lines go to stdout only; stderr stays clean on success.

`bash test_sedbatch.sh` from the workspace root is the acceptance gate; it must pass unmodified — don't touch the test file.
